exemplar.title: How do different types of sugar (sucrose (disaccharide) and glucose (monosaccharide)) (5g) affect the rate of cellular respiration in the yeast S. cerevisiae (11g)?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do different types of sugar (sucrose (disaccharide) and glucose (monosaccharide)) (5g) affect the rate of cellular respiration in the yeast S. cerevisiae (11g)?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question embedded in a specific and appropriate biological context.
Key methodological factors (e.g., temperature, sugar mass, yeast mass, replicates, control, COâ sensor) are identified.
Step-wise procedures provided with quantities allowing reproduction with few ambiguities.
weaknesses: Rationale for some methodological choices (e.g., 5 g sugar) is not experimentally justified.
Omissions in control replication and missing details (chamber volume, sensor calibration, sampling rate) hinder completeness.
Ambiguity in Experimental Group 2 description (e.g., sucrose vs. glucose, truncated temperature notation).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The proposal excels in embedding the research question in a tightly focused context (band 5) but shows only moderate depth in explaining methodological considerations (band 3) and reproducing the procedure with minor ambiguities (band 3). Averaging these strands yields a mid-band performance, warranting 3/6.

strengths: Comprehensive raw data tables with averages and a combined graph.
Basic uncertainty treatment through standard deviation and percentage calculations.
Evidence of data processing relevant to the research question (means, SD, r-values).
weaknesses: Units are inconsistently labelled (ppm mis-denoted as â%â) and table formatting is cluttered.
Treatment of uncertainties omits instrument precision, calibration error and propagation; conceptual errors in interpreting SD as absolute uncertainty.
Graph lacks error bars and axis labels; regression analysis lacks context (no equations, p-values).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of recording and processing is clear or precise but not both (band 3), while consideration of uncertainties is very limited with significant omissions (band 1) and data processing contains inconsistencies (band 3). Overall performance falls in the lower mid-band, supporting 2/6.

strengths: Conclusion relevant to the research question and linked to observed COâ production trends.
Attempts to situate findings within scientific context via literature on invertase activity.
weaknesses: Minor logical leaps and lack of quantitative detail reduce full consistency with analysis.
Scientific context comparison is brief and lacks depth (quantitative benchmarking against published rates).
Contains an incorrect enzymatic statement regarding phosphofructokinase in yeast.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands sit solidly in the mid-band (3â4); the conclusion is relevant but not fully consistent with all analytical details (band 3), and context comparison is relevant but superficial (band 3). Constrained by total-mark requirements, a score of 2/6 reflects this mid-band performance.

strengths: Identification of specific methodological weaknesses (sensor positioning, residue contamination, temperature drift).
Proposed improvements are realistic and clearly linked to stated limitations (e.g., standardising procedures, enhanced sterilisation).
weaknesses: Lacks quantification of the relative impact of each methodological weakness on data quality.
Improvements are described rather than analytically explained in terms of expected error reduction or accuracy gains.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands on methodological weaknesses and improvements both align with band 3â4 descriptors: specific but without analytic depth (band 3). Reflecting a solid mid-band performance across both strands and the need to fit the total of 9, the criterion is awarded 2/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states that sucrose produced more COâ and links this to invertase activity; the conclusion aligns with the processed data and acknowledges that it contradicts the initial prediction.  Minor logical leaps (e.g. control behaving inconsistently) and lack of quantitative reference prevent a fully justified, seamless argument, so the work is best described as **ârelevant but not fully consistent.â**

Large raw-data tables, averages and one combined graph are supplied; headings, units and trial labels are mostly present, though ppm is repeatedly misâlabelled as â%â and some tables are cluttered.  Thus communication is **clear OR precise but not both**, placing it in the 3â4 band.

Relevant processing (means, SD, percentage âuncertaintyâ, r-values, combined graph) is carried out, but several **inaccuracies** occur: ppm/% confusion, correlation calculations unexplained, no rate calculation per unit time, misuse of SD.  Consequently the strand sits in the middle band with âsignificant omissions or inconsistencies.â

Specific weaknesses are identifiedâsensor repositioning, soap residue, temperature driftâwhich shows more than generic listing.  The student does not analyse **relative impact** (e.g. magnitude of COâ error), so explanation depth is limited, corresponding to band 3-4 (low).

The conclusion is compared to literature on invertase and phosphofructokinase, with citations to Barnett, Marques et al., and Gancedo.  These connections are **relevant but brief**; depth of analysis (e.g. quantitative comparison with published COâ rates) is missing, matching the 3â4 band descriptor.

The student embeds the question in a **specific biological setting**: the biochemistry of S. cerevisiae fermentation, the role of invertase, the expected effect of sugar structure and the optimal fermentation temperature.  Background information is not simply generic; it is tightly linked to the chosen organism, sugars, temperature range and measurement method, satisfying the descriptor of a âspecific and appropriate context.â

Uncertainty treatment is limited to SD and a percentage derived from SD/mean; instrumental precision, calibration error, replicate variability and propagation are ignored.  Calculations contain conceptual flaws (SD used as absolute uncertainty, wrong interpretation of percentages).  This is **limited evidence with significant omissions**, fitting band 1-2 (low end).

Improvements (better cleaning/sterilisation, standardised sensor placement, increased control of variables) are **directly linked** to the stated weaknesses, but the rationale is descriptive rather than analytical (no discussion of how much accuracy would rise).  Thus the strand fits band 3-4.

The report **lists major methodological factors** (temperature, sugar mass, yeast mass, replicates, control, use of COâ sensor) and briefly rationalises a few of them (e.g. 33 Â°C to avoid enzyme denaturation).  However, links between these factors and the **need to obtain relevant + sufficient data** are only partially articulated; discussion of sensor range, chamber volume, trial length, or sampling frequency accuracy is absent.  Hence the considerations are described but not fully explained.

Step-wise procedures for each group are provided, together with quantities, but there are **noticeable ambiguities** (e.g. bullet 1 of Experimental Group 2 mentions sucrose instead of glucose, trial duration is stated only later, chamber volume unspecified).  A competent reader could reproduce the work with some clarification, meeting the descriptor âreproduced with few ambiguities,â i.e. mid-band.

annotations.comment: The research question is embedded in a specific biological context, linking sugar structure, yeast mass, temperature and COâ measurement method. This demonstrates a clear, focused research design.
The definition of ppm as âparticles per minuteâ is incorrect; ppm denotes parts per million. Clarify units and ensure consistent, scientifically accurate terminology.
Rationale for using 5 g sugar is based on packaging rather than experimental need. Explain why this quantity is physiologically and analytically relevant to the research question.
Variable control table omits chamber volume, sensor calibration and sampling rate considerations. Include these methodological factors to ensure sufficient data collection.
The description for glucose trials in Experimental Group 2 is cut off (â33Â°C (.â). This ambiguity hinders reproducibilityâcorrect and complete all procedural steps.
Control is only run once while experimental groups have three replicates. For rigorous comparison, replicate the control equally to improve reliability.
Justification for 20 s sampling uses ATP turnover rates but does not directly link to COâ sensor response; clarify why this interval captures fermentation kinetics accurately.
In the sucrose data summary you use â(9/6)â in parenthesesâthis fraction is unclear and inconsistent. Remove or clarify any nonstandard notation.
Claiming qualitative and quantitative data were both collected is misleading; observations are minimal. Focus discussion on quantitative trends or expand qualitative methods.
Uncertainty treatment uses SD directly and ignores propagation and instrument precision. Incorporate systematic uncertainties and propagate errors for a robust analysis.
Uncertainty formula variables (% uncertainty = Î´A/AÃ100%) are mislabelled in text (âSAâ and âA Â· Meanâ). Align notation consistently.
Interpreting 35.5 % uncertainty as 64.5 % accuracy misapplies statistical reasoning. Report precision without converting to âaccuracyâ percentages.
Raw data tables are cluttered and omit units in headers (e.g., ppm). Improve clarity and precision by standardizing table formatting and labelling.
R-value interpretation lacks context on regression model and does not consider slope or significance testing; include regression equations or confidence intervals.
While r-values are all <1, absence of p-values or degrees of freedom makes the strength of correlation ambiguous. Include statistical significance measures.
The graph lacks error bars and axis labels with units, limiting interpretation of variability. Add these elements to improve precision and clarity.
The Results section ends mid-sentence. Ensure completeness and coherence of the narrative, tying observations back to the research question.
The claim that S. cerevisiae lacks phosphofructokinase is incorrect; this enzyme is present and essential in yeast glycolysis. Review enzyme roles in fermentation.
Qualitative observations (color, texture, bubbles) are listed but not linked to fermentation performance. Integrate these details into the analysis or omit them.
Identified weaknesses (sensor repositioning, soap residue) are specific but lack discussion of their relative impact. Quantify potential errors to strengthen evaluation.
Proposed standardization of procedures is relevant but lacks detail on how variability will be quantitatively reduced. Explain specific steps and expected improvements.
Reference list entries lack consistent formatting (APA, MLA, etc.); standardize citation style to improve scholarly presentation and traceability.
